dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 75 responding for breathing problems in Richmond at Apt 422.
Hi, I'm Jake, an EMT with the rescue squad. What led you to call 911 today?
I'm having trouble breathing for about a while now.
Can you tell me your name right now? Just to confirm you're awake and responsive.
What time is it today? I want to make sure you have your bearings.
And the day of the week? This helps us assess your awareness.
If you can, give me a thumbs-up with your left hand.
Your airway appears clear and patent. I don't see any signs of obstruction.
Let me quickly check your breathing for any signs of distress or labored effort.
Pulse is 94 and strong. Your skin looks warm and dry with no signs of cyanosis.
Blood pressure is 144 over 85. Let me check O2 and pulse rates next.
Respiration is 28, a bit fast but regular.
Oxygen saturation is at 90%. I'll patch into full vitals now.
Can you tell me what medications you're taking now?
You have been having trouble breathing-was anything here today making it harder, like activity or cold air?
Can you tell me how much oxygen you're on at home and how you're adjusting it?
You mentioned a history of severe COPD with a portion of your left lung gone, and also prostate cancer. Is your current shortness of breath worse than usual?
The chart shows no significant pain reported-so we'll skip pain assessment for this case.
I'll quickly check your skin for any signs of pallor, jaundice, or other changes.
I'm going to check your neck for swelling or unusual pressure.
Just relax while I listen to your lungs again.
Uneven breath sounds are present, especially with some wheezing on the left side.
EKG now shows a sinus rhythm with signs of atrial enlargement and conduction delay.
Given the signs of COPD exacerbation, I'll proceed with protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress.
Blood sugar is not available at this time. Let me confirm that the tech addressed it.
We're going to try a nebulizer with albuterol. Would that work for you right now?
Unable to place an IV in your extremity vein. We'll continue with alternatives.
Starting the nebulizer now. If it feels uncomfortable, let me know.
Blood pressure is now 153 over 97.
Albuterol hasn't improved the breathing much. I'm going to quickly recheck breath sounds.
Pulse is at 95, respirations are 36, and oxygen remains at 4 LPM via nasal cannula.
I've placed you on a 4-lead ECG to monitor for changes in heart rhythm.
Oxygen saturation has improved slightly to 92%. We're making slight progress.
You're used to using albuterol regularly. Has this pattern occurred before?
Conducting a 12-lead ECG now to get a more detailed cardiac read.
You're under hospice for COPD. Who is your medical power of attorney, and are they okay with transporting to a hospital?
The patient will be placed carefully onto the stretcher with a five-point harness for maximum safety during transport.
Blood pressure is now 151 over 98. It's trending higher but appears stable.
ECG still shows sinus rhythm, left and right atrial enlargement, and the conduction delay is consistent.
I'll try again to start oxygen at 4 LPM via nasal cannula.
Pulse is now 90, respirations are 16, and O2 saturation is 99%. These signs are much more stable.
All steps under protocol 3-11 have been completed and approved by the medical POA.
Final readings: BP 161 over 102, pulse 90, respirations 16, and saturation 99%.
One dose of albuterol, 5 mg via nebulizer, was given.
We'll now perform a sheet lift to transfer the patient into the ED bed safely.
Patient vitals are stable during transport. Neurological status is intact with GCS 15.
Care transfer was completed with the ED nurse and signed off by the POA.
The patient was transported to Retreat Hospital at the request of the POA, despite being on hospice.
During transport, the patient remained on 4 LPM via nasal cannula and on continuous 4-lead ECG monitoring.
ED has received all updates on the patient's status and prognosis through the medical POA.
Based on this detailed assessment, EMS remains within protocol 3-11 for COPD exacerbation in the presence of albuterol resistance.
Oxygen saturation increased to 93% during the same monitoring period.
Rate of breathing decreased slightly from 39 to 16 after the albuterol treatment.
No jugular venous distension noted. Carotid pulses were symmetric.
The trachea remained straight and midline. No laryngeal damage was observed.
Respiratory rate was clearly documented as high as 39 during the critical observation period.
As per the transportation log, the stretcher harness prevented sliding or falls during all phases of the conveyance.
No major complications arose, and the call was reported despite system difficulties in printing the PCR.
The stretcher remained in use after ED transfer for full compliance with medical POA directives.
Rigidity was not an issue, and skin integrity remained intact without signs of bruising.
No abnormal motor deficits throughout the whole call-consistent with prior conscious level.
Limb tools used for lifting (sheet and stretcher) followed hospital and EMS safety policies.
Patient skin condition remained warm, pink, and dry until final handoff.
No incidents reported during loading and unloading processes.
Your last oral intake was not noted in charting, but direct questions were asked around this while on scene.
No visible wounds or scars on the thorax area that could interfere with respiration.
Both halves of your chest rise similarly and fall on breathing effort measurement.
Blood pressure was 144 over 85 at first monitoring and stable in final numbers.
Based on resistance to albuterol, we avoided any further detonation in the field.
We attempted to start an IV in extremity vein but did not achieve success in the field.
By the end of the call, you were free of clinically recorded pain (PAIN: 0).
The ED reported satisfaction with the monitoring protocol, including EKGs, conducted in the ED following arrival.
The ED nurse accepted and signed off on all findings as per the EKG and charted protocol items.
Because you were A&O x3 and had a valid visit to the ED approved by medical POA, transport was justified and conducted.
ED accepted care transfer while on the same stretcher. No patient injuries or issues occurred during the transition.
We avoided repeat Albuterol due to reported adverse lung response-nebulizer was taken off quickly.
For safety, we returned to 4 LPM breathing support before continuing to the hospital.
All signs returned to stable by final transport observation.
SpO2 was 93% at a specific check-working toward full desaturation reversal.
Using 4 LPM oxygen, the patient demonstrated steady but slow improvement in SpO2 readings.
The medical director recommended transport to Retreat due to the potential for COPD cardiac interaction.
The 5-point harness was utilized from arrival to leaving the scene.
BP remained elevated throughout, highest at 161/102, but without clear sign of acute hypertension.
No cardiac EKG exceptions were found other than the noted enlargements from chronic disease.
Patient was in breathless state when accepted but unaware of the field time, making full evaluation possible.
No digital alarms were necessary-only continuous visual monitoring for fluctuations.
No additional medication hold or transition issues.
Retreat Hospital confirmed readiness with ED for on-site treatment.
Patient was transferred to ED by sheet lift using certified protocols for high-morbidity cases.
Family was present during handoff. Per EMS notes, they wanted continued oversight at the hospital in the ED.
No long-term delayed effects were observed during transport or on scene.
All containers and gear were accounted for after scene departure.
The stretcher was secured to the vehicle using standard running gear.
Patient was not dropped or moved unnecessarily during conveyance.
All movement was slow and carefully timed per EKG findings.
The harness was compared to weight limits from the driver's perspective.
During transport, patient was secured with 5-point harness and did not shift.
No roll-over situations reported due to patient's careful positioning.
Albuterol was only administered once, and the patient did not wish repeat treatment.
Because this is a COPD exacerbation in an end-stage patient, the transport was prioritized in full awareness of prognosis and limitations.
All transfer logs were complete, and handoff documents were shared with ED staff and POA.
Despite the high volume and lack of printed PCR, the call remains valid and full in field documentation for future review.
As per the ePCR documentation, all care was provided without additional reported complications.
The call stays in the context of COPD worsening rather than new cardiac or pain etiology.
Oxygen use was maintained via N/C for the full duration with no adverse effects until ED arrival.
The patient was mentally intact (A&O x3, knowing name, location, and purpose) during full transport.
No call was made back to the hospital. ED was informed upon arrival.
Upon arrival, the stretcher position provided no hindrance in ED waking or bed transfer.
Joint motion remains simple with soft limbs and full range-of-motion perception by EMS staff.
No skin downfalls or reported pressure lesions.
The time difference between field and hospital was accounted in assessment.
All transport was verified by GPS app and in video data where available.
No repeats of the IV process despite multiple attempts.
All action carried out under protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress standards.
The stretcher was not used past necessary, per field norms.
Oxygen use was critical in maintaining SpO2 at above 90%, then 99%.
Respiratory improvement was clear but slow with O2 and ample rest.
All electronic devices were setup correctly before leaving the scene.
No PVs, JVD, or visible strain.
All plans or changes were reported to dispatch on arrival and departure timing.
